profile.summary: A mid-level Backend Engineer builds and maintains server-side services, APIs, data flows, and integrations used by products and internal systems. They usually own delivery of well-scoped features and fixes, write tests, review code, and help operate services in production. They work closely with other engineers, product managers, designers, and sometimes data or infrastructure teams.

known_challenges.text: Balancing feature delivery with fixing reliability, performance, and technical debt in existing services.
Debugging production issues across logs, databases, queues, and upstream dependencies with incomplete information.
Making design choices that fit current needs without overengineering or creating future maintenance pain.
Handling interruptions from incidents, support requests, and urgent bug fixes that break planned work.
Coordinating API and schema changes with other teams without causing breakages or rollout delays.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role often writes code that must run reliably in production, so confidence in operating and changing services matters.
Mid-level engineers are expected to make solid choices on simplicity, speed, performance, and maintainability.
How they approach failures shows where they need support, patterns, or stronger systems knowledge.
Backend work is often disrupted by bugs and operational issues, which can quietly drain focus and energy.
Backend changes often depend on contracts with frontend, mobile, platform, or data teams.

terminology.meaning: Rules for how software systems communicate.
A specific API path or operation.
The structure of stored data and tables.
A controlled change to database structure or data.
A buffer for processing work asynchronously.
How long a request takes to complete.
How much work a system handles over time.
Storing results temporarily to speed up access.
Automated build, test, and deployment pipeline.

listen_for: Whether they talk about customer impact or only code completion
Whether production issues are described with ownership, avoidance, or blame
How clearly they explain tradeoffs between speed, simplicity, and long-term maintenance
Whether they mention tests, observability, and rollout safety when discussing changes
Shifts from confident technical detail to vagueness around distributed systems, data, or performance
avoid: Treating backend work as invisible plumbing with less product impact than frontend work
Pushing only for feature speed while ignoring reliability, maintenance, and operational load
Assuming a mid-level engineer should already handle vague architecture problems without support
Reading careful technical caution as lack of urgency or low ownership
